title: [DA] Disable the BanerjeeMIV dependence test
author: sjoerdmeijer
description:
The various `findBounds` helpers (e.g. `findBoundsLT`) are suspected to be incorrect because they do not account for potential integer overflow, which can lead the dependence analysis to produce incorrect results. Since these helpers are used by the BanerjeeMIV dependence test, this patch disables BanerjeeMIV by default to avoid unsafe results.

Specifically, this change removes BanerjeeMIV from the set of dependence tests enabled by default by introducing a new default value for the `da-enable-dependence-test` option, which excludes BanerjeeMIV. All dependence tests, including BanerjeeMIV, can still be enabled with the `all` option, or BanerjeeMIV can be selected explicitly using `BanerjeeMIV`.

I haven't fixed up all tests yet, because wanted to get some early feedback on these two things:
1) Instead of removing the code, I have a minor preference to disable
   this dependence tests as that is easy to do and allows one to still
   experiment with the Banerjee test.
2) It is leading to quite a few regressions in tests, which can be fixed
   up in two ways: add option -da-enable-dependence-test=all to those
   RUN lines, or accept the changes in the dependence relations. Except
   for the Banerjee tests, I guess the latter might be a bit better?

This is addressing/fixing: #169813

target datalayout = "e-p:64:64:64-i1:8:8-i8:8:8-i16:16:16-i32:32:32-i64:64:64-f32:32:32-f64:64:64-v64:64:64-v128:128:128-a0:0:64-s0:64:64-f80:128:128-n8:16:32:64-S128"

  %conv5 = trunc i64 %j.01 to i32
  %arrayidx = getelementptr inbounds i32, ptr %B, i64 %j.01
  store i32 %conv5, ptr %arrayidx, align 4
  %inc = add nuw nsw i64 %j.01, 1
  %exitcond = icmp ne i64 %inc, %2
  br i1 %exitcond, label %for.j, label %for.end

for.end:                                          ; preds = %for.j
  %inc.lcssa = phi i64 [ %inc, %for.j ]
  %3 = mul nuw nsw i64 %indvars.iv6, %0
  %arrayidx6 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %A, i64 %3
  %arrayidx7 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %arrayidx6, i64 %inc.lcssa
  store float 1.230000e+02, ptr %arrayidx7, align 4
  %wide.trip.count = zext i32 %add to i64
  br label %for.k

for.k:                                            ; preds = %for.k, %for.end
  %indvars.iv = phi i64 [ 0, %for.end ], [ %indvars.iv.next, %for.k ]
  %4 = trunc i64 %indvars.iv to i32
  %conv13 = sitofp i32 %4 to float
  %5 = mul nuw nsw i64 %indvars.iv6, %0
  %arrayidx15 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %A, i64 %5
  %arrayidx17 = getelementptr inbounds float, ptr %arrayidx15, i64 %indvars.iv
  store float %conv13, ptr %arrayidx17, align 4
  %indvars.iv.next = add nuw nsw i64 %indvars.iv, 1
  %exitcond5 = icmp ne i64 %indvars.iv.next, %wide.trip.count

  ret void

comments.author: Meinersbur
comments.body:
>  are suspected to be incorrect because they do not account for potential integer overflow,

Not opposing this PR, but doesn't this currently apply to all tests in DA? Why single out BanerjeeMIV ?

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
> > are suspected to be incorrect because they do not account for potential integer overflow,
> 
> Not opposing this PR, but doesn't this currently apply to all tests in DA? Why single out BanerjeeMIV ?

Yeah, that is a good question that also crossed my mind. I didn't mention this explicitly in the description, but wanted to discuss that too.

I started with Banerjee, because the Banerjee MIV test is the more complicated one, and is probably not necessary for most cases (if we improve Delinearization).

But if we are deconstructing DA, and building it slowly up again step by step, we could start with just having the ZIV and SIV tests enabled, and disable all the other ones. So yes, I agree with asking the question. Any ideas or preferences on this?

comments.author: Meinersbur
comments.body:
> So yes, I agree with asking the question. Any ideas or preferences on this?

If that's you chosen approach, that's fine for me. I am glad that someone looks at it.

However, there are a relatively large number of pass tests failing that seemingly depend on this test. Seems `-da-enable-dependence-test=all` need to be added to them. I would not disable those tests since that would reduce your test coverage when fixing BanerjeeMIV.

Why did you choose to add `-da-enable-dependence-test=all` to some tests, but add TODO to others?

comments.author: sjoerdmeijer
comments.body:
Hi @kasuga-fj , @amehsan , @Meinersbur ,
Here's a sign of life; I have been less active upstream because I am prototyping the full flow starting from Fortran code (Flang) to interchanging a loop nest in our motivating example (we had too many patches flying around, but not the full flow). The corresponding IR of our motivating example is this one: [test/Transforms/LoopInterchange/large-nested-4d.ll](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/blob/main/llvm/test/Transforms/LoopInterchange/large-nested-4d.ll).

After work in Flang and Delinearization, and correct subscript recognition, there is now only one dependency still in the way. I.e., DA finds an output dependency that shouldn't be there. Extracted from the pseudo code of that motivating example and regression test, it is this store:

     store GlobL(NY*i+j,NY*IL+JL)

These subscripts have multiple index variables, and so I will need a MIV test for this. Thus I am kind of pre-announcing I will need this test back one day to handle this case.

What I am investigating and fixing at the moment is to provide Banerjee with more information; there are symbols in its upperbound and it can't make the right decisions. So I am not yet working on correct issues here, but first investigating how to support this case.

Hope this extra bit of context helps.

comments.author: kasuga-fj
comments.body:
Just my 2 cents:

I think it's very challenging to handle your motivating example without emitting runtime predicates and performing loop-versioning. Because, extracting meaningful information about `NX` and `NY` from the IR seems to be extremely difficult. Looking at the pseudo code, we can see that both of them are less than 54. However, only with the IR, even proving something as simple as `NX <s INT32_MAX` may require a significant amount of work.

Well, for example, (assuming 0-indexed arrays) by some control-flow analysis we can prove the store `store GlobL(NY*i+j,NY*IL+JL)` is executed when `(i, j, IL, JL) = (NX-1, NY-1, NX-1, NY-1)`, and we know the size of `GlobL` is `1000*1000`. The offset of the access is `NY*i+j + 1000*(NY*IL+JL)`, so we can derive the inequality `(NY*(NX-1)+(NX-1)) + 1000*(NY*(NX-1)+(NX-1)) < 1000*1000`. I tried to [feed this to z3](https://gist.github.com/kasuga-fj/c7217754662a933735b5ed80c14bda3d), and apparently there is no loop-carried dependency. But there is a term `NX*NY`, which is quadratic, so DA may not be able to handle them (probably even Polly cannot?). But without this restriction, there exists a solution...
